steps.title: Different problem from K-cuts
steps.narrative:
Refactor and resub want a single, larger cut at each node — not the full k-feasible cut set the rewrite/mapper enumerate. Their cut is grown greedily outward from pNode, picking at each step the leaf whose expansion best preserves "reconvergence" — i.e., paths that re-merge inside the cone.

Reconvergent leaves are good targets because expanding them tends to keep the cone size bounded (multiple paths merge back rather than fanning out wildly).

steps.title: Initial leaf set: just the fanins of pNode
steps.narrative: Start with L = {fanin0(f), fanin1(f)} = {n8, n9}. The cone is the single node f. Each leaf gets a cost: for fanout-1 nodes the cost prefers expansion (cheap, no new sharing); for higher-fanout leaves expansion would push them out of the cone.

steps.title: Iter 1: expand n8 (cheapest leaf)
steps.narrative: n8 has fanout 1 (only f uses it) → cheap to absorb. Pop n8 from L, push its two fanins. New leaves: {n6, n7, n9}. Cone gains n8.

steps.title: Iter 2: expand n9 (also fanout 1)
steps.narrative: n9 fans out only to f → cheap to absorb. Pop n9, push its fanins {c, d}. New leaves: {n6, n7, c, d}. d was already a fanin of n7 — it doesn't appear twice; it's a set.

steps.title: Iter 3+: shall we expand more?
steps.narrative: Both remaining fanout-1 leaves (n6, n7) could be absorbed cheaply. Expanding n6 = a∧b would push {a, b} into L; expanding n7 = a∧d would push {a, d}. Note that expanding either creates the leaf a — and since a is also produced by the other expansion, the leaves reconverge. That is exactly the situation the cost function rewards: expanding both keeps |L| small (a is shared, not duplicated).

entries.Cone: { f, n8, n9, n6, n7 } — the whole f-cone

panes.title: Why stop here?
panes.body: All remaining leaves are PIs. PIs cannot be expanded (no fanins). The procedure terminates.

steps.title: Result for refactor / resub
steps.narrative: The single returned cut is L = {a, b, c, d} with cone {f, n8, n9, n6, n7} — 4 leaves, 5 internal nodes. Refactor will compute the truth table over L; resub will use the cone's internal nodes as a divisor pool. The leaf-and-cone bounds (nNodeSizeMax, nConeSizeMax) protect against pathological growth on bigger circuits.

panes.body: Larger circuits. On real designs the bound |L| ≤ 10 stops growth long before reaching PIs; the cost function biases toward leaves whose expansion shrinks or preserves |L| via reconvergence.
